mainText:
**The Rugby Match Drink-Up**
April 4, 2026
By Scott Salvato
After 80 minutes of organized violence, two rugby teams cheer for each other, shake hands, and head out for the real test: the post-match drink-up.
The post-match drink-up is as vital to rugby as the scrum. Held for both teams and their fans, it is hosted by the home side. In the rugby-playing nations of the world, the drink-up occurs in a comfortable clubhouse established generations ago, supported by thousands of retired players who remain integral members of "The Club." In contrast, most American rugby drink-ups happen in Irish pubs or dive bars. For college clubs, venues sometimes include the dilapidated basements of off-campus houses. Often, they settle for a nearby clearing or parking lot. Although American rugby has left behind the traditional clubhouse of the old world, it has gained a certain soul.
Players from rugby nations visiting the U.S. often have mixed reactions to the American post-match drink-up. Some are disappointed by its simplicity, while others revel in the nostalgic feel of returning to rugby's mid-19th century days—a time dedicated to the love of a game notorious for its roughness. Many foreign players prefer the straightforwardness of American drink-ups to their polished clubhouses back home.
In Britain, there's a saying that soccer is a gentleman’s game played by hooligans, while rugby is a hooligan’s game played by gentlemen. On-field fights are accepted but never brought into the drink-up environment. Carrying a grudge into a drink-up is considered taboo.
A rugby drink-up resembles a medieval Norse hall where warriors joyfully delight in the gashes and bruises they've inflicted on each other. I recall one Saturday in the Bronx when a sizeable competitor stood on my head with both cleats while I was pinned to the ground. Another time a player from Rockaway Beach nearly drop-kicked a goal off my face. An hour later, we were sharing jokes and beers. Time remains suspended on the rugby pitch, and even two decades later, I might owe those players a debt of gratitude in an old boys’ match.
After I retired from playing, I founded a college rugby club while serving as a campus minister. Initially, my players were oblivious to the sport's culture, so I refrained from mentioning the drink-ups. However, their new friends on opposing teams quickly filled them in on the customs, including the infamous rugby songs.
Rugby players are well-known for their hospitality. After the 9/11 attacks, when tensions were high around air travel, over 20 of us boarded a flight in New York City wearing our rugby jerseys and team jackets. A silent plane erupted with smiles as we entered. A woman exclaimed, "Thank God, rugby players!" The plane broke into laughter and applause. The drink-up serves as a warm, living incubator of global brotherhood.
Friends, family, and children are always welcome at the drink-up. Hostilities and bravado are strictly prohibited. Guests enjoy the protection of a sacred camaraderie. When food is served, visiting teams eat first, and many relationships including romances have flourished in this uniquely warm and cheerful setting.
If you've never attended a rugby drink-up, no need to worry. Simply search for the nearest local rugby club and catch a game from the friendly sidelines. When the match concludes and players cheer their opponents and shake hands, just ask, "Where’s the drink-up?" Someone will provide the address, and another will offer a ride. Fellowship and camaraderie will surely follow. Just be cautious — it’s habit-forming. I came for the rugby and stayed for the brotherhood; it may just change your life.
